caption:
JavaScript just got a new keyword: using

It auto-cleans resources when they go out of scope.
No more try/finally. No more forgotten .close() calls.

This changes how you write JS in 2026.

📌 SAVE this — you'll need it soon.

#javascript #typescript #es2025 #coding #programming #webdev #frontend #backend #nodejs #newfeature #codingtips

slides.question: Which is cleaner?
slides.subtitle: Managing database connections in JavaScript

option_a.label: try/finally (old way)

option_b.label: using keyword (new way)

slides.cta: One of these is the future. Swipe →

slides.title: B is the future of JS.

cards.label: Option A — try/finally
cards.title: Works, but fragile
cards.text: Developers forget .close() constantly. If an error throws before finally, resources leak. It's boilerplate that shouldn't exist.

cards.label: Option B — using keyword
cards.title: Automatic cleanup. Zero boilerplate.
cards.text: Part of the TC39 Explicit Resource Management proposal (Stage 3). Works in TypeScript 5.2+ today. Coming to V8. The resource's [Symbol.dispose]() method runs automatically.

slides.takeaway: Like Python's 'with', C#'s 'using', or Go's 'defer' — but for JavaScript.

slides.title: Build your own disposable
slides.intro: Any object with Symbol.dispose works with using:
slides.explanation: Define [Symbol.dispose]() → use using → never leak resources again.

slides.title: using keyword cheat sheet

items.text: await using → async disposal via Symbol.asyncDispose
Perfect for DB connections, file handles, locks, timers

slides.prompt: Will you use this in your next project? 👇
